fact: July 7 is exactly 188 days into the year, which means there are exactly 177 days left - that's almost perfectly balanced!
In the Northern Hemisphere, fireflies are typically at their flashiest around this time, blinking like tiny disco balls in backyards every night.
The sun rises super early on July 7 in places like Alaska - around 4:30 AM, so kids there could watch the sunrise before most people even dream about waking up!
This date falls in the middle of Cancer season, making it one of the most 'crabby' days of the year according to the zodiac.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, July 7 is deep winter, so while Northern Hemisphere kids are sweating in summer, kids on the other side of Earth are experiencing cold weather.
Mosquitoes are typically at their most active around this time in many places because the hot, humid weather makes them breed in any standing water.
The Earth is actually at its farthest point from the sun around this time of year, called aphelion - so even though it's blazing hot in summer places, we're literally as far from the sun as we get!
In many northern forests, this is often peak berry season when wild blueberries and raspberries are bursting with juice and ready to stain your fingers purple.
July 7 sunsets in northern summer locations can seem to last forever because the sun sets at such a shallow angle, creating epic orange and pink skies.
Cicadas are usually screaming their loudest around this date in many places, making that buzzing sound that can reach 120 decibels - louder than a chainsaw!
This is typically prime thunderstorm season in many parts of the world, when hot air collides with cool air to create massive, towering clouds that look like giant cotton castles.
In the Arctic, July 7 means the sun literally never sets - it just circles around the sky all day and night like a glowing ball.
